schools.summary: The sinless Christ prays not from need but to teach us diligence by example (Cyril); the disciples seek a fuller, more petitionary form than the Jewish doxologies (Lightfoot).
Henry: Luke specially notes Christ praying often; 'teach us to pray' is itself a needful prayer, for only Christ can teach us to pray well.

The Lukan form reproduces the Sermon on the Mount with verbal variation (Ellicott); its brevity (vs Matthew) shows it a directory and model, not a rigid form — though Wesley holds we should use this very form.

'Our Father' the grace of adoption — sons made of revolted servants (Pseudo-Augustine, Cyril of Jerusalem); 'in heaven' raises the hearer up (Theophylact); 'hallowed be thy name' prays it be sanctified in us and known to the world; 'thy kingdom come' puts down all sin and rival rule.
The petitions reproduce Matthew with variation; Augustine notes Luke's brevity (the third petition a repetition of the former two), though the better texts include 'Thy will be done.'

themes: Our Father which art in heaven

schools.summary: 'Daily bread' — the literal bread of daily need, teaching contentment and honest poverty (Cyril, Chrysostom), trusting God not self (Basil); we ask only the day's necessities, taking no thought for the morrow.
Pseudo-Augustine/Titus: the Greek epiousios ('added to the substance') points to the spiritual bread of everlasting life — the word, the Eucharist, the food of the soul.

debates.question: Is 'daily bread' literal bread or spiritual/eucharistic bread?

sides.position: Literal daily food — teaching contentment and dependence
Spiritual 'supersubstantial' bread — the word/Eucharist (the Greek epiousios)

schools.summary: Forgiveness asked on the reciprocal condition of forgiving others — God made the imitator of our patience (Cyril); 'deliver us from evil' = 'the wicked one,' belonging (Augustine) to the temptation petition.
Forgiving others not the meritorious cause but the disposition without which God cannot pardon (Barnes, Wesley); 'deliver us from the wicked one' (the better rendering, Clarke, Lightfoot).
'Lead us not into temptation' = not be overwhelmed by it (the excellent swimmer passes through, Cyril of Jerusalem); we pray not to be abandoned to our temptations (Titus).

themes: deliver us from the wicked one

schools.summary: The friend at midnight (peculiar to Luke, Ellicott) teaches importunity — if human persistence extorts a favour, how much more will God answer perseverance (Calvin, Barnes); no time unseasonable with God (Wesley).

Cyril: cowardice in prayer hurtful, patience advantageous; Augustine reads an allegory — three loaves (the heavenly mystery/Trinity), the friend on a journey (this transient life), midnight (the famine of the word).
The benighted travelling friend whom the host cannot feed makes the case urgent (Clarke); the empty cupboard the occasion of the midnight request — conscious need the motive of importunate prayer.
Augustine (allegory): the friend 'in his journey' is this present life of travellers; the host's emptiness drives the believer to seek from God the food he cannot supply.

The reluctant friend refuses (the door shut, family abed, 'I cannot' = 'I am not willing,' Robertson), yielding only to persistence — an à fortiori foil to God's readiness: if a grudging man yields, how much more the willing Father who knows our needs (Ellicott).

'Importunity' = shamelessness, pertinacity that knows no restraint (Ellicott, Robertson; cf. Abraham, the Syro-Phoenician woman); applied to prayer (not making God reluctant): God delays to try faith and deepen dependence, so we persevere 'again and again' (Barnes).

Ask/seek/knock distinguished as rising stages — petition, diligent zeal, persevering continuance (Chrysostom, Greek Exposition); the promise has the force of an oath (Cyril), for God is more willing to give than we to receive (Augustine).
The asking must be right — those who ask amiss, for vain ends, do not truly ask (Origen, James); importunity brings us into the disposition to receive (Clarke).

The promise restated as a universal rule — 'every one' who asks rightly receives, whatever his nation or rank; the exceptions ask amiss (Gill); the answer comes at once or 'in God's due time,' the delay a trial of faith, not denial.
The argument from human fatherhood (an awkward but intelligible construction, Robertson) — no good father gives a stone for bread or a serpent for fish; the ground of confidence in prayer.
Cyril of Alexandria: God, like a wise father, withholds even what we ask amiss when it would harm us, giving only what is truly good.

The egg/scorpion comparison (a folded white scorpion resembling an egg, Barnes) completes the picture — no good father gives his child a venomous thing for the nourishing one asked; the fatherly goodness that gives good and withholds harm.

Luke's 'Holy Spirit' (for Matthew's 'good things') names the highest gift, the fullness of all blessings (Robertson), perhaps spoken to the Seventy (Ellicott); the beautiful gradation friend-father-God (Wesley) assures the Father's willingness.
Even the Apostles 'evil' compared to divine goodness, God alone good (Bede); the Holy Spirit the fullness of God's gifts, and (Athanasius) of God's own substance, since he is called good.

themes: being evil, know to give good gifts
how much more the heavenly Father
the Holy Spirit the best gift

schools.summary: The dumb (and blind, per Matthew) spirit the cause of the soul's deafness to God's word (Titus); its casting out figures the Spirit's work in conversion — the blind see, the dumb speak God's praises (Bede); the blessed effect of the Spirit promised in v.13 (Gloss).
Christ casts out the dumb demon, the freed man speaks, the crowd wonders — but the miracle becomes the occasion of the Pharisees' blasphemy (v.15) and the demand for a sign (v.16).

The Pharisees, unable to deny the miracle, ascribe it to Beelzebub the chief of demons — 'a most absurd explanation' (Robertson); the name (Beelzebul, 'lord of flies,' god of Ekron, Lightfoot, Wesley) the Jews' title for the prince of demons; the charge the blasphemy against the Spirit.
Calvin: the demand for a sign a pretext for unbelief — having seen abundant miracles, they demand a heavenly portent not to believe but to excuse not believing, alleging Christ's calling unattested.
A second group demands a spectacular sign from heaven (Robertson), realizing the Beelzebub charge's futility; the wickedness of an unbelief no evidence can satisfy.

Christ answers their thoughts, not their words (that they acknowledge his power, Bede), from everyday experience (Chrysostom): no divided kingdom or house stands — if Christ cast out demons by Satan, Satan's power would perish.
Ambrose/Bede: Christ's kingdom (the Church, the undivided Trinity) is everlasting because undivided, while unbelieving Israel, denying its Messiah, cannot stand; against the Arians.
Christ's omniscience reads their thoughts (Robertson); the vivid image of one tumbling house knocking down its neighbour.

The argument applied: if Satan is divided against himself his kingdom cannot stand — the charge refutes itself, for Satan does not war against Satan (Gill); Christ holds them to their own words ('because ye say,' Robertson), a reductio ad absurdum.

The argument from the Jewish exorcists ('your sons') who cast out demons and credit it to God — if theirs is God's work, Christ's cannot be Satan's; the Pharisees condemn in Christ what they approve in their own, so their sons judge them (Bede, Chrysostom).
For argument's sake Christ assumes the charge, a reductio ad absurdum (Robertson); the Pharisees' inconsistency (the Jewish exorcists, Acts 19:13) turned against them.

themes: by whom do your sons cast them out

schools.summary: 'Finger of God' = 'Spirit of God' (Matthew) — the Spirit called the finger for the distribution of gifts (Augustine), consubstantial with the Son as the finger with the hand (Cyril); the miracle proves the kingdom has come, enriching human nature.

'Finger of God' the concentrated, directed power of God (Ellicott, recalling Exodus 8:19; 31:18); 'the kingdom is come upon you' present in power, but 'upon' the unbelieving in judgment (Titus, Robertson).

themes: the kingdom of God is come

schools.summary: The 'strong man armed' is the devil, holding undisputed dominion before Christ — his 'palace' the world in wickedness, his 'arms' all sins, his 'goods in peace' the captive souls (Cyril, Theophylact, Bede); his strength borrowed from human weakness, not natural (Chrysostom).
The devil armed with his temptations and men's lusts (Gill), strong in the pride and security of those he dwells in (Wesley); his 'court' his homestead (Robertson).

'A stronger than he' is Christ, who overcomes Satan, strips his armour (sin), and divides his spoils — the captive souls rescued (Cyril, Bede); the triumph of Christ, leading captivity captive and giving gifts (Ephesians 4).
Christ the mighty God, manifestly stronger than Satan (Gill); strong as Satan is, Jesus wins victories over him, triumphing openly by the Cross (Robertson, Colossians 2:15).

No neutrality toward Christ — 'he that is not with me is against me'; not aimed at those merely not yet disciples, but at those who tolerate the blasphemy, neither defending Christ nor opposing his accusers (Gill); indifference is enmity.
Christ gathers the scattered sons of God, Satan scatters them (Cyril, Chrysostom) — in this conflict there is no middle ground.

themes: he that is not with me is against me

schools.summary: The returning spirit applies primarily to the Jewish nation — the spirit of idolatry cast out by the Law, but rejecting Christ they receive a worse possession (Cyril, Ambrose; fulfilled under Titus, Chrysostom); also to the apostate individual who neglects good works (Bede).
Almost verbatim with Matthew 12:43-45; the cast-out spirit wanders restlessly and resolves to return (Robertson).

debates.question: Does the returning spirit apply to the Jewish nation or the apostate individual?

sides.position: Primarily the Jewish nation (per Matthew's 'this wicked generation')
Also the apostate or relapsing individual

themes: I will return to my house

schools.summary: The house 'swept and garnished' — cleansed of former filth and outwardly adorned, yet empty of goodness (Gill), purified by baptism but not filled with good works (Bede); the emptiness invites the spirit's return; applied to the Jewish nation, even 'heated' with wrath against Christ.

The cast-out spirit returns with seven worse — applied to the Jewish nation's worse possession, raging against the Lord of the Prophets and ruined under Titus (Chrysostom); the seven spirits the whole accumulated attack opposed to the Spirit's sevenfold gifts (Ambrose).
The worse last state a warning of apostasy (Wesley, citing Josephus; Barnes, Hebrews 6:4; 2 Peter 2:20) — relapse after enlightenment more desperate than the original condition.

themes: the last state worse than the first

schools.summary: Bede: the woman's blessing confesses Christ's true incarnation, refuting both the Pharisees' blasphemy and later heretics who denied Mary gave of her own substance to the Son; Christ directs the blessing higher (v.28) — his birth profited her nothing without faith and works (Chrysostom).

Peculiar to Luke, 'the Gospel of Woman' (Robertson), from the devout women's company (Ellicott); fulfils Mary's prophecy 'all generations shall call me blessed' (1:48); Henry: a check to doting on Christ's bodily presence.

themes: blessed is the womb that bare thee
blessedness in hearing and keeping the word

schools.summary: Jesus turns attention to a wider beatitude, its originality guaranteeing its historical character; 'the word of God' the message of the kingdom (Matthew 13:20), including the written word.
Wesley: even Mary's blessedness rested on hearing and keeping the word, else she would have forfeited it.
Bede: he answers the sign-seekers; Ambrose: the sign of Jonah a type of the Passion, both warning and mercy; Theophylact: Jonah converted Nineveh, but the Jews believed not at Christ's rising.
The rare verb 'were gathering together'; 'shall be given' implies the resurrection sign (Matthew 12:39); Augustine harmonises the differing order in Matthew and Luke.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the Beelzebub charge (11:19-26), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: The original account (Jonah 1:17; 2:10); Jonah a sign by his three days in the whale and deliverance.
Calvin: 'sign' denotes not an ordinary token but the miraculous — Jonah brought from the fish's belly as from the grave to call men to repentance.
Cyril of Jerusalem (on point): Jonah three days in the whale, the Son of man three days in the heart of the earth.

themes: the Son of man to this generation

schools.summary: The queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1); Lightfoot defends the plain sense against the rabbinic 'kingdom'; she is set with the men of this generation, who would not hear a greater than Solomon.
Solomon and Jonah in the ablative after 'greater'; Luke sets the Queen of the South between the two mentions of Jonah; 'men of this race shall rise' (Clarke).
Wesley: Nineveh repented only for a season and was destroyed after forty years — so God reprieved the Jews about forty years before their ruin.

outliers.note: The note here concerns a genealogy, not this verse.

schools.summary: Cyril: against the charge that Christ worked miracles for applause, his light is not to be hid.
Bede: though only Jonah's sign was given, Christ's light is not hid from the faithful; Gregory: the lamp of a good intention lighting the action.
'In a cellar' (krypten, only here); the parallels (Matthew 5:15; Mark 4:21); Lightfoot connects it to the sign-seekers.

Theophylact: as the bodily eye governs the body, the understanding governs the soul; Origen: the understanding desires singleness without guile; Chrysostom: corrupt it and you do violence to the whole soul.
'The light of the body is the eye' (Matthew 6:22); the understanding the light and guide of the soul; the moral sense closer in Luke (Ellicott).

themes: the eye the lamp of the body

schools.summary: Better 'see to it whether the light in thee be darkness', a call to self-scrutiny of one's first principles by a higher Light; the idiom 'whether not' (me) in indirect question, a pitiful situation if the light is darkness.

The apparent truism is the most expressive truth — the whole life illumined by the divine light is wholly bright; 'with its bright shining' (te astrape), as by a flash; bringing a candid mind (Lightfoot).
Clarke and Wesley: the light of Christ dwelling fully in the heart extends to every thought, word, and action, like a clear flame lighting a room.

themes: the whole body full of light

schools.summary: Bede: 'as he spake these things' shows he was interrupted; Augustine: the Pharisee's unspoken thought heard by Christ, who spared him by reproof; Chrysostom: almsgiving makes all clean.
'To dine' (aristesē) the morning/noon meal, not supper; the invitation perhaps malignant, to draw Christ from the people (Barnes); Henry: he said privately what he later said publicly (Matthew 23).

outliers.note: The block here comments on the sign of Jonah (11:29-36), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: The washing of hands, not the whole body (Lightfoot); 'washed' literally 'baptized' (immersed), the Pharisaic standard (Mark 7:3-4); the custom arose from eating with the hands (Barnes).
Christ speaks of the persons, not the vessels — 'he that made that which is without' is God (Lightfoot); 'ravening' (harpages, plunder) and wickedness within (Matthew 23:25).
Calvin: Christ glances at their hypocrisy, desiring purity only before the eyes of men.
'Fools' denotes want of wisdom and wickedness; God who made the body made the soul, both must be clean; the question implies that ceremonial and moral law have the same Maker (Ellicott).

themes: one Maker of body and soul

schools.summary: The crux ta enonta — Robertson: 'the things within' given as alms with inward righteousness; Lightfoot reads it ironically; Barnes: 'such things as ye have', even if ill-gained, given in repentance; Clarke: what is within or at hand.
Calvin: Christ withdraws them from ceremonies to charity — not water but liberality cleanses, not disparaging God's ceremonies but rebuking those who think God amused by signs.

debates.question: What does 'give alms of such things as ye have' (ta enonta) mean?

sides.position: The things within the dishes / inward righteousness given as alms
Such things as ye have, even if unjustly gained, given in repentance
Spoken ironically against the Pharisees' overrating of alms

schools.summary: 'Tithe' (apodekatoute) of rue and every herb, where Matthew 23:23 has anise and cummin; Luke's 'the love of God' for Matthew's 'mercy and faith'; these they ought to have done without leaving the others.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the cleansing of the cup (11:37-41), a catena offset, not this verse.
A catechetical passage on the Spirit, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: 'The chief seats in the synagogues' (the bench facing the congregation), singular here, plural in Matthew 23:6, a marked Pharisaic trait (Luke 20:46); each affected to be a ruler (Clarke).
The 'tombs which appear not' (adela) gave defilement for seven days (Numbers 19:16), hence whitewashed as a warning; here unmarked, distinct from the whited sepulchres of Matthew 23:27.
Wesley: Christ likely eyed the scribes, the graves overgrown so men stumble and are defiled unawares.
Cyril: a reproof exalting the meek is hateful to the proud; Bede: grievous the conscience that thinks the word of God a reproach against itself.
Theophylact distinguishes the lawyers (interpreters of the Law) from the Pharisees (a sect).
'Thou reproachest us also' (hybrizeis, an insult); the lawyers usually Pharisees, learned in the law, standing or falling with the scribes.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the woes against the Pharisees (11:42-44), a catena offset, not this verse.

schools.summary: 'Grievous to be borne' (dysbastakta); a fierce indictment of the lawyers' pettifogging oral interpretations (Matthew 23:4); the burdens the yokes of tradition they would not touch (Lightfoot).
'Ye build' = rebuild and beautify the tombs (Matthew 23:29); Luke omits the 'sepulchres of the righteous'; the woe on lawyers, Pharisees, and the nation (Gill).

themes: honouring the dead, rejecting the living

schools.summary: 'Consent' (syneudokeite, full approval, as Saul to Stephen's death, Acts 8:1); building tombs as if disapproving, but opposing what the prophets taught; 'allow' = approve (Romans 1:32).
Wesley: pretending reverence for the prophets while destroying God's present messengers, they witness they are of the same spirit as their fathers.

'The wisdom of God' — Barnes/Clarke: the Saviour himself (1 Corinthians 1:24), who in Matthew 23:34 says 'I send'; Robertson: God in his wisdom said, Jesus knowing the divine counsels; Lightfoot's rabbinic parallel.
Wesley: what God's wisdom has said in Scripture — prophets under the Old Testament, apostles under the New.

debates.question: Who is 'the wisdom of God' here?

sides.position: Christ himself, the wisdom of God (cf. Matthew 23:34, 'I send')

schools.summary: 'May be required' (ekzetethē, as a debt), 'shed from the foundation of the world'; the differing phrase from Matthew's 'righteous blood' (Matthew 23:35); 'that' (hina) may mark result, not design.
Wesley: the blood would be visibly and terribly punished on that generation.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage, tangential to the verse.

themes: the blood of all the prophets

schools.summary: Abel the first blood shed (Genesis 4:10), Zacharias the last in the canon, which ended with Chronicles (2 Chronicles 24:22) — Genesis to Chronicles; Luke omits 'son of Barachias' (the perplexity of Matthew 23:35), favouring a later addition there.
Wesley: fulfilled within forty years in the destruction of temple, city, and nation.

debates.question: Which Zacharias, and why no 'son of Barachias' in Luke?

sides.position: Zacharias son of Jehoiada (2 Chronicles 24); Luke's omission favours 'son of Barachias' as a later addition in Matthew

outliers.note: A catechetical passage on eternal life, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: A flat charge of obscurantism — they refused to enter, locked the door, hid the key (Matthew 23:13); they took the key to themselves teaching trifles (Lightfoot); each scribe admitted by the delivery of a key (Ellicott).
Wesley: obscuring the knowledge of the Messiah, the key of the kingdom.

outliers.note: The block here comments on the lawyers' burdens (11:45-48), a catena offset, not this verse.

themes: the key of knowledge taken away

schools.summary: The better text 'when he had gone forth'; 'to press upon' (enechein, to have it in for, used of Herodias, Mark 6:19) and 'to provoke to speak' (apostomatizein, to draw out impromptu); they pressed many questions to entangle him.
Calvin: Luke places these latest reproofs in connection with the dinner, though spoken near the end.

outliers.note: A catechetical passage, tangential to the verse.

schools.summary: 'Laying wait' (enedreuontes, to lie in ambush) and 'to catch' (thēreusai, to ensnare in hunting), treating Jesus as prey; the better text 'to catch something out of his mouth', foreshadowing the tribute-question (Matthew 22:15).
